narrative: Today's feed is dominated by evolution and molecular ecology, community ecology and conservation. The evolutionary standout shows summer rainfall driving local adaptation despite gene flow in a widespread butterfly, complemented by work separating plasticity from genetic divergence across climate gradients. Climate-change responses centre on a 5,151-species global resurvey finding temperate local extinctions now outpacing tropical ones, and on upslope range squeezes in mountain birds and mammals. Conservation and community papers cluster on restoration and coexistence — from wheat–weed niche differentiation to nutrient-driven food-web destabilisation and Pleistocene baselines for functional-diversity loss. Fire is smaller but pointed: an Australian trait-based framework for rapid fire-impact assessment of data-poor invertebrates, plus evidence that post-fire resprouting buffers drought. Methods are unusually busy, led by a herbarium-AI surge of new identification and classification tools and a flurry of airborne-environmental-DNA work, alongside new tools for acoustic monitoring, camera-trap density and pollinator detection. Population, movement, biogeography, plant traits and theory each contribute a few high-signal papers, including forest connectivity collapse in the Amazon and a 25-year reckoning for modern coexistence theory.
